responseSet.title: Anna Freud: A Biography By Elisabeth Young-Bruehl.  London:  Macmillan.  1988.  Pp. 527.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anna Freud

A Biography By Elisabeth Young-Bruehl. London: Macmillan. 1988. Pp. 527.

Clifford Yorke

Clifford Yorke

Clifford Yorke
21 Maresfield Gardens, London NW3 5SH

Clifford Yorke  

W. H. Auden referred to biographers as 'gossipwriters and voyeurs calling themselves scholars'. And, addressing the claim that knowledge of a man's life throws light on his works, Auden wrote that: 'Biographies of writers are always superfluous and usually in bad taste', and argued that 'no knowledge of the raw ingredients would explain the peculiar flavour of the verbal dishes that the writer's public were invited to taste'. He declared emphatically that the writer's 'private life is, or should be, of no concern to anybody except himself, his family and his friends'.

Freud's views on biography were every bit as forthright. Addressing Arnold Zweig in a letter written shortly after his eightieth birthday, Freud expressed alarm at the threat that the novelist wanted to become his biographer. Making plain his surprise, he added with no little vigour: '—you, who have so much better and more important things to do, you who can establish monarchs and who can survey the brutal folly of mankind from a lofty vantage point; no, I am far too fond of you to permit such a thing. Anyone who writes a biography is committed to lies, concealments, hypocrisy, flattery and even to hiding his own lack of understanding, for biographical truth does not exist, and if it did we could not use it'. And many years before, in telling his fiancée Martha Bernays of his decision to destroy all his papers except for family letters, he added: 'Let the biographers labour and toil, we will not make it too easy for them'.

In the nature of things, biographers are not in the least disheartened by the opinions of their subjects. Auden's biographer, Humphrey Carpenter, was well aware of, and quoted from, Auden's objections, but argued his way round them and set his course undeterred. As for Freud, the biographies, the books about him, the reams of discourse since the authorized Life and Work by Ernest Jones, have now become a flood. The quality varies from the serious and academic to the plainly silly; a great deal is mischievous and even malign. But even the more substantial works give reasons for public disclosure of private material that are often disingenuous and sometimes downright sophistry. Even Peter Gay, who has a reputation as a serious and academic historian, and whose recent biography has claims to critical status, shows himself a dab hand when it comes to self-justification, and would have done better to skip the casuistry that mars his preface and simply get on with the job.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Special Symbols in Early Female Oedipal Development: Fantasies of Folds and Spaces, Protuberances and Concavities

Anita Schmukler and Emanuel Garcia

Emanuel Garcia

Anita G. Schmukler
Emanuel E. Garcia
PHILADELPHIA
7900 Old York Road, Suite 109A
Elkins Park, PA 19117

Emanuel Garcia  

SUMMARY

The authors present three examples, from the observation of pre-latency girls, of the use of anatomic regions for symbolic purposes. In the two cases, the first interdigital fold became the locus for castration anxiety; and in the other, the laryngeal prominence and jugular notch served as the focus for symbolic displacement of psychosexual concerns. The employment of these particular symbols, to the best of the authors' knowledge, has not been reported previously in the literature. The authors conclude by calling for further investigation into the relationship between the processes involved in such symbol-formation and those that occur in neurosogenesis.

responseSet.title: The Politics of Psychoanalysis: By Stephen Frosh.  New Haven and London:  Yale University Press. 1987.  Pp. 290.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Politics of Psychoanalysis

By Stephen Frosh. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1987. Pp. 290.

Sara Tucker

Sara Tucker

Sara S. Tucker
2701 Fairfax Road, Cleveland Heights, Ohio 44118

Sara Tucker  

In this well-organized, readable book, Stephen Frosh focuses on Freudian and post-Freudian theory as it relates to social thought, particularly the relationship between social structures and individuality. The title does not refer to manoeuvrings for power within the psychoanalytic movement; rather, by 'politics', the author refers to 'the positions taken up with respect to the power relations that exist between people and that are embedded in the major structures of society' (p. 12). He also explores the application of psychoanalysis to wider political questions.

Examining psychoanalytic theory from the viewpoint of power relations makes for controversial and, at times, biased readings, as evidenced by Frosh's dismissive appraisal of Anna Freud's contribution to psychoanalysis and his unsupported criticisms of capitalism. Still, Frosh does bring to bear a thorough mastery of his subject as well as a talent for homing in on the key elements of various theories, then phrasing them so they can be understood by a wide audience. In doing this, however, he sacrifices the essential links to the clinical underpinnings of the formulations, so that the theory may appear arbitrary or fanciful. While this probably would not present a problem for psychoanalysts, it does require a certain suspension of belief on the part of readers from other disciplines.

For those psychoanalysts not familiar with the critiques of thinkers outside the field of psychoanalysis, this book is excellent as an introduction to the political arguments the divergent psychoanalytic theories are used to validate or deny. Further, even those who are familiar with these disciplines will appreciate the author's commentary, which is woven into the fabric of the presentation in an enlightening way.

As stated by the author, 'this book has a dual purpose: to introduce a complex range of psychoanalytic thought, and to do so in a manner that reveals and assesses its political significance … Without a theory of subjectivity, of how the individual's experience becomes organised, there can be no complete approach to politics. Psychoanalysis, with all its retrogressive tendencies and complex contradictions, offers the most provocative and persuasive prospects for the development of such a theory that are currently available' (pp. 14–15).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Effect of Relationships on Relationships

A conference jointly sponsored by
THE TAVISTOCK CLINIC and
THE WORLD ASSOCIATION OF INFANT PSYCHIATRY AND ALLIED DISCIPLINES
(WAIPAD)

In collaboration with
THE ANNA FREUD CENTRE
In Honour of John Bowlby
London: November 9–10, 1990

The Conference will explore the clinical implications of advances in our understanding of how relationships within the family interact and how this then affects the quality of relationships throughout the lifespan. In a dialogue between researchers and clinicians current clinical practice will be integrated with recent research findings.

Speakers will include: Kathryn Barnard, John Byng-Hall, Peter de Chateau, Robert Emde, Peter Fonagy, Robert Hinde, Serge Lebovici, George Moran, Lynne Murray, Joy Osofsky, Daniel Stern, Joan Stevenson-Hinde, Colwyn Trevarthen, Judith Trowell.

Further details from: Angela Norris
Conference Co-ordinator
The Training Office
The Tavistock Clinic
120 Belsize Lane
LONDON NW3 5BA
England

responseSet.title: The Margaret S. Mahler Literature Prize
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Margaret S. Mahler Literature Prize

The Literature Prize Committee of the Margaret S. Mahler Psychiatric Research Foundation is now accepting papers to be considered for the 1990 annual prize of $750 which will be awarded to the author of an original paper judged to be the best by the Committee.

Papers should deal with clinical, theoretical or research issues specifically related to Dr Mahler's concepts of separation-individuation in child development.

Pre-published papers may be submitted provided that they have been published within the year in which the Prize is awarded. Papers must be received by December 31st 1990 to be considered for this year. The Prize will be conferred at the annual Margaret S. Mahler Child Development Symposium held in Philadelphia in April or May 1991.

Six copies of the paper should be submitted for consideration to:
Marjorie Harley, Ph.D., Chairperson
Margaret S. Mahler Literature
Prize Committee
201 St. Martins Road

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Motivation: By Joseph D. Lichtenberg, with a contribution by June L. Hadley.  Hillsdale, N.J.:  The Analytic Press.  1989.  Pp. 422.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Motivation

By Joseph D. Lichtenberg, with a contribution by June L. Hadley. Hillsdale, N.J.: The Analytic Press. 1989. Pp. 422.

Sterrett Mayson

Sterrett Mayson

Sterrett Mayson
Suite 4B, 8200 Flourtown Avenue, Wyndmoor, PA 19118

Sterrett Mayson  

The history of psychoanalytic thinking about motivation is a history of the core controversies which have occupied the field. Freud's discovery of unconscious sexual motives and his appreciation of their power generated much of the excitement of the early days of psychoanalysis. Freud's struggles to understand masochistic motives contributed to the development of the dual instinct theory. Freud elaborated the structural theory in part because he came to recognize unconscious motives of defence. Ego psychologists organized and systematized the energic and structural concepts implied but not fully spelled out in Freud's various instinctual drive theories of motivation. The last 30 years have seen many efforts at replacing the economic concepts of classical metapsychology with other models. These various models, such as information processing models, have implied different theories of motivation.

Joseph Lichtenberg steps into the fray with the volume under review. Lichtenberg's goal is to present a theory of motivation consistent with the burgeoning data from infant observation studies and also clinically relevant. This effort follows naturally from his 1983 book Psychoanalysis and Infant Research in which he reviewed the infant observation studies available at that time. His current book takes into account the findings of these and later studies and focuses specifically on the question of motivation.

The core of Lichtenberg's thesis is his concept of five motivational systems. The purpose of each system is to fulfil or regulate a basic need. The five basic needs are:

(1) The need for psychic regulation of physiological requirements, (2) the need for attachment-affiliation, (3) the need for exploration and assertion, (4) the need to react aversively through antagonism or withdrawal, and (5) the need for sensual enjoyment and sexual excitement (p. 1).

Lichtenberg devotes much of his book to documenting how each of these motivational systems is consistent with behaviours which have been clearly observed in the early years of life. He believes that the dual drive theory has been inadequate to answer questions of motivation and is not consistent with infant observational findings. He currently points out that research has proved that infants are not passive recipients of internal and external stimuli seeking to discharge tension. Indeed, his theory is based on the now well documented discovery that infants show complex patterns of self regulation and interaction with caregivers from the earliest days of life. A constant emphasis throughout the book is the infant observed not in isolation but rather in the context of the interaction with his caregiver. Lichtenberg argues that in infancy, 'each system contributes to self-regulation in mutually regulatory interactions with caregivers' (p. 1). Lichtenberg is therefore necessarily concerned with the issue of the development of the self and the object. Acknowledging his debt to self psychology, Lichtenberg conceives of the self as 'an independent center for initiating, organizing, and integrating motivation' (p. 1).

responseSet.title: Multiple Realities in Clinical Practice: By John S. Kafka.  New Haven:  Yale University Press. 1989.  Pp. 199.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Multiple Realities in Clinical Practice

By John S. Kafka. New Haven: Yale University Press. 1989. Pp. 199.

Robert Meyers

Robert Meyers

Robert W. Meyers
4524 Forest Park Blvd., St. Louis, MO 63108

Robert Meyers  

Although a shared commonsense reality is the basis of ordinary communication, different reality organizations are seen in normal life, in neurotic patients, and, most strikingly, in psychosis. It is to the origin, development, and clinical understanding of these multiple realities that this book is devoted. However, this wide-ranging work also deals with issues of perception, time, ambiguity, rituals, and animate-inanimate distinction. To the theoretical exploration of a variety of clinical phenomena, John Kafka, an analyst who has had a long experience in the treatment of schizophrenia, applies ideas from philosophy, epistemology, physics, and neurobiology. This is a heady speculative brew which must be read carefully; the propositions, as the author states, are difficult.

Kafka proposes that multiple realities are organized along certain axes; a spatial-temporal axis, a size-of-organism axis (primarily from individual to family or group), and an animate-inanimate axis. The spatial aspect corresponds to the concrete and inanimate whereas the temporal is more related to the psychological and animate. In the temporal context he discusses the centrality of time for many psychoanalytic issues, the most important for the analyst being the restructuring of past experience in the present.

His basic assumptions regarding perception and reality are developed in the second chapter, which is a combination of two of the author's earlier papers. (In fact, substantial portions of this book are almost verbatim reproductions of his previously published papers.) Kafka argues that the vantage point of perception must be integrated into psychoanalytic thinking. He cites a tachistoscopic experiment in which Rorschach cards were briefly shown to adults: the subjects reported seeing what very young children see when they have unlimited time to look at the cards; when adults see the cards for increasingly longer periods, they report what progressively older children see, until at the end, everyday commonsense reality is established. From this it was concluded that each perceptual act recapitulates the ontogeny of perception. This is the key concept to Kafka's understanding of reality.

responseSet.title: Erikson Scholar Announcement
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Erikson Scholar Announcement

The Austen Riggs Center is pleased to once again announce and invite nominations for the position of Erik H. Erikson Scholar at the Austen Riggs Center. This endowed position honors Professor Erikson's transforming contributions to the fields of psychoanalysis, human development and history, and extends that work through supporting the clinical and research interests of a distinguished scholar-in-residence. A rangements include salary, housing, office space, secretarial assistance and daily participation with the ongoing clinical, educational and research work of the Center. Nominations are currently being accepted for tenure of one year or more beginning July, 1992 and beyond. For further information and to submit nominations, please contact: M. Gerard Fromm, Ph.D., Chairperson, Erikson Scholar Search Committee, Austen Riggs Center, Stockbridge, Massachusetts 01262.

responseSet.title: Freud's Self Analysis: By Didier Anzieu.  London:  The Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis.  1986.  Pp. 585+bibliographies and indices.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Self Analysis

By Didier Anzieu. London: The Hogarth Press and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis. 1986. Pp. 585+bibliographies and indices.

Juliet Mitchell-Rossdale

Juliet Mitchell-Rossdale

Juliet Mitchell-Rossdale
275 Lonsdale Road, London SW13 9QP9

Juliet Mitchell-Rossdale  

There are two versions of the original French edition of Freud's Self-Analysis. The English translation uses the second. In both, Didier Anzieu's project is to situate the work of Freud's self-analysis at the very centre of the discovery of psychoanalysis thus making the testing ground for the establishment of the theory also the basis of the therapy that results. But something intervened between the first and second editions which while it does not alter, yet it shifts, or, rather, specifies the project. For two years, with Eva Rosenblum, Anzieu led weekly seminars that each focused exclusively, and in chronological order, on one of Freud's dreams. These detailed analyses went into the second edition. Thus it is the analysis of dreams that gives not exclusive but privileged access to Freud's self-analysis and hence to the discovery of psychoanalysis. Dreams are re-established as the royal road and Anzieu's biography with its fascinating extended interpretations is, in a sense, another 'dreambook'.

Everything else is there: Freud's reading, actual events, cultural history, the letters to Fliess, relations with family and professional friends, illnesses, slips, probable day's residues and associations, but these inform a dominant structure of dream interpretation. A rich documentary account of turn-of-the-century Vienna and of the discovery of psychoanalysis bubbles through this dream analysis—of which it is, of course, a part.

Formally, Freud's Self-Analysis is divided into five sections: Freud's early years (up to 1895), his discovery of how to understand dreams, of the Oedipus complex, of the universal fantasy of the primal scene and his first awareness—as yet unnamed—of castration anxiety. But this linear narrative is punctuated by retrospective reconstructions, proleptic hints and timeless motifs, most importantly on the questions of creativity and destruction. This interplay of times is a replication of a particular mode of analytic session in which through the experience of a timeless here-and-now present, one perceives that the fundamental structure, as in Freud's own case-accounts, is a repeated and varied historical reconstruction. This homology of form and content exemplifies the book's greatness.

Affect Deficit: A Vicissitude of the Phenomenon and Experience of Affect

Daniel Traub-Werner

Daniel Traub-Werner

Daniel Traub-Werner
602-586 Eglinton Avenue East
Toronto
Ontario
Canada M4P 1P2

Daniel Traub-Werner  

SUMMARY

Certain people are unable to discriminate, integrate and regulate their affective experience and their experience of pain. This disorder is related to alexithymia, la pensée operatoire and the dis-affected condition. Affect is best understood when a distinction is made between its somatic emergence and its subjective experience. The two patients described here were unable to provide language, mnemic symbol or associative meaning to their affective experiences. Their analyses revealed long histories of denial, disavowal and metaphorization of their somatic perceptions. The lack of validation of their somatic experiences contributed to their affect deficit condition. From a metapsychological perspective it is here hypothesized that in the above condition the body ego remains partially unintegrated from the sphere of the ego.

responseSet.title: Reshaping the Psychoanalytic Domain: The Work of D. W. Winnicott, W. R. D. Fairbairn, and Melanie Klein: By Judith Hughes.  Berkeley, Los Angeles and London:  California University Press.  1989.  Pp. 244.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reshaping the Psychoanalytic Domain: The Work of D. W. Winnicott, W. R. D. Fairbairn, and Melanie Klein

By Judith Hughes. Berkeley, Los Angeles and London: California University Press. 1989. Pp. 244.

John Padel

John Padel

John Padel
Hinton Waldrist, Oxon SN7 8RN, England

John Padel  

The great value of this book lies in the fact that it is a well-researched piece of work by a professor of history, who ends her preface by thanking her analyst. It has the virtues of a view of psychoanalysis from outside as well as of sympathetic understanding.

The explicit aim is twofold: to make clinicians in the United States and elsewhere who have a piecemeal knowledge of British object-relations theory appreciate its internal coherence, and also to establish such coherence in the work of the three authors named in the title, 'which constitutes … the soundest and the most thoroughgoing revision of Freud'. The book will therefore be read rather differently by British psychoanalysts and by others. Others will ask if it describes a convincing and useful 'revision of Freud'; in Britain it will be examined by the different groups for whether it gives an accurate and fair account of the theories and clinical work of the authors and whether it does establish a coherence between them.

Since the book was already launched when Jay Greenberg & Stephen Mitchell's Object Relations in Psychoanalytic Theory was published in 1983, it has been at least six years a-making. Considering the enormous number of books and papers cited in the Bibliography, valuable in itself, of over 30 pages and over 700 items, this is not a long time. Those sources have been supplemented by tape-recordings and interviews made in Britain, as relevant evidence of what the author-theorists 'themselves thought they were doing'. So those interviewed were people who had greater or less personal knowledge of Klein, Fairbairn, or Winnicott and of their work and writings. The material so gained was accumulated over a rather brief time; such journalism, like rescue-archaeology, is a valuable way of recording facts from sources which are soon about to perish or to be destroyed, but, unlike rescue-archaeology, it is sometimes correctable from the same or other sources.

One item here that is in urgent need of correction is Hughes' assertion that Fairbairn succumbed 'to a combination of depression, drink, and Parkinson's disease' (p. 17). He did not: he had arteriosclerosis and apparently several strokes, some Parkinsonism, but normal liver-functions. The clinical records make no mention of alcohol. Those who saw him near the end did not describe an unusual depression but even a gentle humour with which he spoke of the short time left to him. Hearsay on such a matter as the cause of a death requires careful checking.

responseSet.title: Freud. Appraisals and Reappraisals, Volume 3: Edited by Paul E. Stepansky.  Hillsdale, NJ:  The Analytic Press. 1988. Pp. 201.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud. Appraisals and Reappraisals, Volume 3

Edited by Paul E. Stepansky. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1988. Pp. 201.

Joel Whitebook

Joel Whitebook

Joel Whitebook
21 East 12th Street, New York, NY 10003

Joel Whitebook  

Before turning to the volume under review, I would like to situate the '[the] renaissance of Freud studies' (p. 3), of which it is a part, in its larger historical and theoretical context. Two of the great 'masters of suspicion' (Ricoeur, 1970, p. 33) Marx and Freud, sought to unmask the ilusory nature of our various mental products, e.g. morality, religion, art etc., by explaining their manifest content in terms of a more fundamental substratum, i.e. material conditions and drive-related wishes. They did not, however, turn the weapons of suspicion against themselves. (The third master of suspicion, Nietzsche, did make such a move, and, in this respect, fundamentally differs from the other two.) Marx and Freud remained relatively confident, if not somewhat complacent, about the validity of the sciences they employed to conduct their critiques of illusion, and never attempted to subject their own standpoints, which of course were themselves products of the mind, to systematic critique.

However, this sort of 'radicalized critique of reason' (Habermas, 1987, p. 302), which has become '[suspicious] of the masters of suspicion' (Rorty, 1985, p. 161), and attempts to debunk them with their own critical weapons, has become prevalent today. It can be observed in the various forms of 'postmodern' philosophy (e.g. Derrida, Foucault, Lyotard, Winch, Rorty and Feyerabend), which has become deeply sceptical about the foundations of Western rationality and science, and, at times, in the new philosophy and history of science as well. Because he is both a major figure in the history of Western science and, at the same time, a master of suspicion, Freud is an inviting target for debunking critique. The aim is to show that Freud's rationalism cannot be maintained against the consequences of his own discovery of the unconscious; his own discovery, in other words, threatens to swamp the very scientific ground from which it was made.

The Kantian tradition in the philosophy of science has always insisted that the question of a theory's validity (the level of verification) be kept completely insulated from considerations concerning the factual conditions that generated that theory (the level of discovery). This insistence is born out of the fear that the introduction of genetic questions into the consideration of a theory's validity could not only jeopardize the validity of a particular theory, but, as a generalized approach, could threaten the very notion of validity as such. Thus, rather than attempting to understand the very complex and fascinating relation between genesis and validity, they phobically excluded the topic from their investigations altogether.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Treatment: An Intersubjective Approach: By Robert D. Stolorow, Bernard Brandchaft and George E. Atwood.  Hillsdale, NJ:  The Analytic Press. 1987.  Pp. x + 187.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Treatment: An Intersubjective Approach

By Robert D. Stolorow, Bernard Brandchaft and George E. Atwood. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1987. Pp. x + 187.

Christopher Meyers

Christopher Meyers

Christopher D. Meyers
3525 Prytania Street, New Orleans, LA 70115

Christopher Meyers  

Psychoanalysis is a discipline that examines data generated in a two-person group comprised of a patient and an analyst. The authors of this book are intrigued by the fact that each participant sees himself and his world in a unique way. In other words, each of them has his own subjectivity. The authors have focused upon the interaction of these subjectivities, and have termed this focus the intersubjective approach. This approach provides a platform from which to examine and comment on various aspects of psychoanalytic theory and practice.

The authors are convinced of the superiority of the intersubjective viewpoint and have written a forthrightly polemical work in defence of it. In highlighting differences between their views and those of other psychoanalysts, they tend towards simplistic descriptions of other theoretical positions, and one is left at times with the impression that the authors have been attacking straw men. This is unfortunate; readers who are put off early in the book may neglect a number of valuable clinical presentations which follow. Regrettably, the clinical material is impossible to summarize adequately here, and our emphasis must be upon the authors' theoretical statements.

An interesting puzzle appears early in the argument. On the one hand, the authors assert that all psychoanalytic understanding takes place within the context defined by a particular patient and a particular analyst. On the other hand, they allow themselves to refer, for example, to 'forms of psychopathology, ranging from the psychoneurotic to the overtly psychotic', or to 'the situation that arises in treatment when there is a wide but unrecognized disparity between the relatively structured world of the analyst and an archaically organized personal universe of the patient'. Even these ordinary generalizations would seem to be ruled out by the authors' insistence that they 'do not believe that the analyst possesses any "objective" knowledge of the patient's life or of human development and human psychological functioning'. Such radical epistemological modesty would seem to make any general statements about psychoanalysis and, a fortiori, any reference to a patient's archaically organized universe impossible. The authors do not appear to recognize the unresolvable paradox entailed by their position.

responseSet.title: Understanding Countertransference: From Projective Identification to Empathy: By Michael J. Tansey and Walter F. Burke. Hillsdale, NJ:  The Analytic Press. 1989.  Pp. 222.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Countertransference: From Projective Identification to Empathy

By Michael J. Tansey and Walter F. Burke. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1989. Pp. 222.

James Frosch

James Frosch

James P. Frosch
875 Massachusetts Avenue, Suite 54, Cambridge, Mass. 02139

James Frosch  

After a period of relative neglect, the study of countertransference has finally come into its own—with a vengeance. Many observers have noted that the development of attitudes towards the countertransference has followed a similar path as those towards the transference. Initially regarded as regrettable and disruptive, it was then viewed as still regrettable but potentially useful, and finally as inevitable and essential to the therapeutic process. During this type of evolution excesses inevitably occur, and we can expect there will be those who regard the countertransference as not just an important thing but everything, and that eventually a balanced consensus will develop, a consensus which does justice to the complex, multifaceted nature of the analytic process. In the midst of the current excitement, it is sobering to re-read some of the papers from the first flowering of countertransference literature in the early 1950s, and to find that most of the current questions were well known and hotly debated by analysts thirty years ago (Orr, 1954).

Then as now, the first difficulty is that, while we all agree that the countertransference is important, few of us can agree on what it is. Some prefer to use a restricted definition, reserving the word to describe the analyst's unconscious displacement of a figure from his own life on to the patient, while others broaden the definition to include all reactions towards the patient, both conscious and unconscious, both those stemming primarily from the analyst's conflicts and those primarily evoked by the character and behaviour of the patient. Those few who agree on a definition are likely to disagree on other matters, such as how best to utilize the countertransference and whether to reveal it to the patient.

Most current papers on the topic do not contribute anything new to these old debates, but rather restate the familiar questions. It was therefore a pleasure to read this book, in which the authors both critically review the prior literature and propose some novel ways of looking at old problems. While there is little that will be entirely new to the experienced analyst, the book's lucidity and balance will make it a valuable aid in teaching and will help clarify the technical principles that guide our work.

responseSet.title: Repetition and Trauma: Towards a Teleonomic Theory of Psychoanalysis: By Max M. Stern. Hillsdale, NJ:  The Analytic Press.  1988.  Pp. 169.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Repetition and Trauma: Towards a Teleonomic Theory of Psychoanalysis

By Max M. Stern. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1988. Pp. 169.

Ernest Hartmann

Ernest Hartmann

Ernest Hartmann
27 Clark Street, Newton, MA 02161

Ernest Hartmann  

In this short but ambitious work, Stern makes a valiant effort at a very difficult task. He wishes to integrate psychoanalytic views of human development, mastery of trauma, etc. into a 'teleonomic' (goal-directed) theory of evolution, based on Monod's concept of 'autonomous morphogenesis'—summarized by Stern (p. 142) as:

A living being's structure owes almost nothing to the action of outside forces, but everything, from its overall shape down to its tiniest detail, to "morphogenetic" interactions within the object itself. It is thus a structure giving proof of an autonomous determinism: precise, rigorous, implying a virtually total "freedom" with respect to outside agents or conditions—which are capable, to be sure, of impeding this development, but not of governing or guiding it, not of prescribing its organizational scheme to the living object (pp. 10–11).

Monod's view bridges the 'innate' and the 'acquired':

When behavior implies elements acquired through experience, they are acquired according to a program. That program is innate—that is to say, genetically determined. The program's structure initiates and guides learning, which will follow a certain preestablished pattern defined in the species' genetic code.

This goal is elucidated only in Stern's last chapter. The building blocks of human psychic development which he wants to use are not always made entirely clear. One central emphasis is Stern's equation of teleonomy or morphogenesis, at the psychological level, with gradual mastery, which involves 'attributing meaning to one's own states of tension'. He sees arrests in development as failure to attribute meaning to one's own states of tension: he finds this situation in pavor nocturnus and also in the repetitive phenomena of the transference neurosis. In fact, he tries to equate the two in some sense. He states in his last chapter, 'I should like to propose that the state of tension to which meaning is not attributed in the transference neurosis is precisely the inhibition of physiological activity observed in pavor nocturnus'. And further, this 'state of primary physiological depression (as in pavor nocturnus) is itself the depression observed upon interpretation of reparative mastery in treatment'.

London:  Unwin Hyman.  1989.  Pp. 240.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Flanders, Sara" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Sara</nfirst><nlast>Flanders</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: Decoding Your Dreams: By Robert Langs.  London:  Unwin Hyman.  1989.  Pp. 240.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Decoding Your Dreams

By Robert Langs. London: Unwin Hyman. 1989. Pp. 240.

Sara Flanders

Sara Flanders

Sara Flanders
73 Ronalds Road, London N5 1XB

Sara Flanders  

Robert Langs' Decoding Your Dreams announces itself boldly on the cover as 'A Revolutionary Technique for Understanding Your Dreams'. The claim raises a question that is never quite adequately answered in the text. Where, one wonders, will this well established and widely published psychoanalyst locate the revolution? In 1900 Freud published 'The interpretation of dreams'. Its message, that the dream has meaning, can be interpreted, is the 'royal road to a knowledge of the unconscious activities of the mind', has been debated, embraced, and elaborated within the psychoanalytic establishment and without ever since. But Langs makes only a few references to Freud, none to any other psychoanalysts, none to the dream research of the last decades, and meagre reference to the revolutionary volume of nearly a century ago. Apparently Langs' imaginary audience is devoid of historical or scholarly interest, nor is it bothered to know his own intellectual credentials, which are in fact considerable. He seems confident of his book finding readers who come freshly to the idea of dream interpretation, but who would, at least after his enthusiastic and sometimes dazzling introduction, apply themselves earnestly and usefully to the contemplation of their own dreams.

Contrary to the appreciative comments published on the dust jacket, Langs' message is not a straightforward popularization of current psychoanalytic understanding. His theme, that the dreamer is the best interpreter of his own dream, implies that anyone with a wish to fathom its meaning is better off telling no one, friend or professional. Such an approach is only revolutionary within the psychoanalytic establishment, to which Langs does not address himself here. Without quite openly confronting his colleagues, he declares little or no confidence in the dream telling process which has been a cornerstone of psychoanalysis since its beginning. He advises the dreamer to value, remember and analyse his own dreams, but not to communicate them, as if intrapsychic and interpersonal communication were antithetical. The other person will get it wrong, will project his meaning on to the dream, will render it meaningless and useless in one way or another. Here, Langs, the exasperated supervisor, makes his way into the text. In the current intellectual climate, arguably better disposed to the study of the dream than to psychoanalysis, Langs' approach may claim the rationale of expedience.

responseSet.title: London Weekend Conference for English-Speaking Members of European Societies 12–14 October 1990
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

London Weekend Conference for English-Speaking Members of European Societies 12–14 October 1990

Registration will take place on Friday, 12th October 1990, between 6.30 and 9 p.m., at North and South Cloisters, University College London, Gower Street, London W.C.1. The Conference will be held on Saturday and Sunday (a.m. only), 13th and 14th October 1990, at The Logan Hall, Institute of Education, 20 Bedford Way, London W.C.1.

The topic of the Conference is:

THE TREATMENT ALLIANCE AND THE TRANSFERENCE

There will be three papers on work with adults on Saturday given by Mrs Ignes Sodre, Dr Sheilagh Davies and Mr Christopher Bollas. These sessions will be chaired by Dr Mervin Glasser, Dr Jonathan Pedder and Mrs Elizabeth Spillius respectively. Mr Peter Fonagy will be opening the discussion of the paper by Mr Bollas.

There will be two papers on the application of the theme of the Conference to work with children and adults on Sunday given by Miss Betty Joseph and Mrs Anne-Marie Sandler. This will be followed by a session of dialogue between them and Dr Nian Coltart.

There will be a meeting of students on Sunday afternoon.

The Programme and application forms will be despatched mid-April to all members of the EPF automatically. Thereafter further copies may be obtained from Madeline Garfield (Miss) at The British Psycho-Analytical Society, 63 New Cavendish Street, London W1M 7RD.

responseSet.title: Developmental Breakdown and Psychoanalytic Treatment in Adolescence: Clinical Studies: Edited by Moses Laufer and M. Eglé Laufer.  New Haven and London:  Yale University Press.  1989.  Pp. 217.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developmental Breakdown and Psychoanalytic Treatment in Adolescence: Clinical Studies

Edited by Moses Laufer and M. Eglé Laufer. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1989. Pp. 217.

Geoffrey Baruch

Geoffrey Baruch

Geoffrey Baruch
4 Rochester Road, London NW1 9JH

Geoffrey Baruch  

This book comprises a description of nine severely disturbed adolescents in five times per week psychoanalysis undertaken by analysts at the Centre for Research into Adolescent Breakdown. The clinical work was part of a research project. This research project sought to understand the nature and severity of breakdown in adolescence by distinguishing three types of adolescents according to the way they used their body to express their disturbance. They included the adolescent whose relationships were of a perverse nature, the adolescent who had attempted suicide and the adolescent whose behaviour showed signs of severe disturbance in functioning such that his relationship to external reality was clearly distorted (e.g. as in anorexia nervosa).

The Laufers consider these different uses of the body as the outcome of defences which are mobilized by the adolescent whose relationship to his sexual body breaks down at puberty. As the adolescent develops, he finds that these defences are unable to cope with the anxieties he experiences about his maturing sexual body. Usually, it is at this point when self destructive attacks against the body take place that professional help is sought. In summary, the Laufers maintain that the key to understanding adolescent breakdown is the uncovering of the adolescent's relationship to his sexual body at the time of puberty which they claim is subsequently repeated and lived out in the transference.

In order to understand these formulations, which perhaps may be unfamiliar to many analysts not acquainted with patients who break down in adolescence, I think it is helpful to state some ideas from the Laufers' first book Adolescence and Developmental Breakdown1 since these ideas clearly influence the thinking behind the research project. In this book they state that;

During adolescence oedipal wishes are tested within the context of the person's having physically mature genitals, and a compromise solution is found between what is wished for and what can be allowed. This compromise solution … defines the person's sexual identity … We therefore see the main developmental function of adolescence as the establishment of the final sexual organization—an organization which must now include the physically mature genitals. The various developmental tasks of adolescence should be subsumed under this main developmental function rather than viewed as separate tasks (p. 5).

responseSet.title: The Myth of Athena and Arachne: Some Oedipal and Pre-Oedipal Aspects of Creative Challenge in Women and their Implications for the Interpretation of Las Menin As by Velazquez
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Myth of Athena and Arachne: Some Oedipal and Pre-Oedipal Aspects of Creative Challenge in Women and their Implications for the Interpretation of Las Menin As by Velazquez

Laurie Adams

Laurie Adams

Laurie Adams
63 East 93rd street
New York
NY 10128

Laurie Adams  

SUMMARY

This paper interprets the myth of Athena and Arachne, as told by Ovid, as a metaphor for mother-daughter conflict. It discusses pre-oedipal and oedipal aspects of the myth with particular focus on creative challenge and artistic competition. A fragment of a clinical case in which the transference exhibited dynamics inherent in the myth is presented. It is proposed that the analyst's knowledge of myth can be helpful in understanding transference manifestations. Finally, Velazquez's Las Meninas is

607

discussed in the light of the myth, which is the subject of the small painting on the back wall above and to the left of the mirror. The author argues that, just as myth can elucidate psychoanalysis, so psychoanalysis can elucidate myth and its inclusion in an artist's iconography.

responseSet.title: A Journey to Pain
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Journey to Pain

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan
JERUSALEM
2 Mohaliver Street
76304 Rehovot
Israel

Ilany Kogan  

SUMMARY

This paper explored the restoration of the capacity to feel pain and guilt as a result of the emotional experience in analysis. This was illustrated by the first part of an analysis of a woman who lost her four-month-old baby in a car accident. Analysis revealed in this case a fragmented self unable to deal with unbearable feelings of pain and guilt which threatened to annihilate it. She connected the accident to a series of events which appeared to her to be the realization of her aggressive wishes towards primary objects throughout her life. The perception of the impairment and loss of loved

638

objects as due to her destructiveness led to the inability to differentiate self from object, the impairment in the work of mourning, and aggression turned against the self. The analyst's survival of the destructive attack without retaliation enabled the differentiation, a better adaptation to reality and the restoration of the capacity to feel pain and guilt.
